narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 48% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50—below the typical 3.21 average—your bottom-decile rankings in both Year 1 and Year 3 school grades, a solid but slightly above-average LSAT score of 37, high family income, and your status as a full-time male White student, which aligns closely with common applicant demographics. The model weighed these factors carefully, with your low Year 1 decile ranking having the strongest influence, pushing the prediction notably toward expecting a bar failure. Next in impact was your similarly low Year 3 decile ranking, which reinforced that concern by suggesting ongoing academic challenges. Your LSAT score followed closely, slightly nudging the odds toward failure despite being a bit better than average. High family income ranked fourth in influence, contributing in a way that didn't offset the risks as much as hoped, while your undergraduate GPA came right after, highlighting how your lower grades there amplified worries about bar readiness. Your racial background as White, being the most common in our dataset, had a moderate pull toward the failure prediction, with gender and full-time status having the smallest effects, both leaning slightly against passing. Overall, these academic markers—especially your early and later school deciles—stood out as the top drivers, far outweighing demographic or background elements. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your grades and GPA are in the lower range, which pulled your profile below the stronger academic baselines that predict first-try success. The good news is some of these can be addressed: focus on boosting your study habits and academic performance now, perhaps through targeted prep courses or retaking undergrad-level assessments if possible, to build a track record of improvement. For the bar specifically, prioritizing rigorous practice with past exams and seeking tutoring in weak subjects could shift future predictions in your favor. You're close in areas like LSAT and family support—lean on those strengths while tackling grades head-on, and reapply after demonstrating progress. We believe in second chances; keep pushing, and you'll improve your odds.
